summary: O'Neil uses dieting as a case study to demonstrate proper statistical thinking, showing a hierarchy from useless anecdotal evidence to meaningful population-level studies. She argues that personal success stories about dieting are statistically meaningless when 97% of people in rigorous studies fail to lose weight. The post also dismantles the 'willpower' theory of weight loss by using herself as a counterexample—someone with extraordinary discipline who remains heavy. She suggests that her early dieting attempts, enabled by willpower, may have actually caused metabolic damage that made her heavier long-term.
key_points: There's a vast difference between 'this worked for me' and 'this would work as public health policy'—most people conflate the two
The gold standard for diet advice should be: can you transform a population of morbidly obese people to normal weight? (Current answer: no)
Studies tracking all participants, not just completers, show 97% of people lose no weight and many gain weight
The willpower theory of weight loss is contradicted by people with extreme discipline who remain heavy
Early dieting interventions may cause lasting metabolic damage, making the 'solution' part of the problem
money_quotes: What I'd love is for people to understand how much difference there is between a personal experience (1) and advice we'd have on public health (5).
If you can come up with something to tell Medicare about how to have a population of morbidly obese people become a population of regular weight people, then you win.
If you're tempted to tell me about a lifestyle change that worked for you, please don't, because that's not statistical.
Readers, I'm the fucking center of the disciple in willpower universe over here.
I'm guessing I'd be thinner if I'd had less willpower, in other words.
I went on a fat-free diet, on the advice of my doctor, that fucked me up; I lost 30 pounds but then gained something like 75.
key_insight: Individual success stories about weight loss are statistically meaningless noise that obscure the population-level reality: we have no intervention that reliably helps most people lose weight.
